data.title: Charter Communications: Fear Reaches Extreme Levels (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Charter Communications, Inc. (NASDAQ:CHTR) plunged over 15% on Friday after the company reported weaker quarterly results, which intensified concerns about cord-cutting and even broadband losses. With this drop, shares have essentially wiped out all the gains they had enjoyed over the past year, with enthusiasm over its pending deal with Cox Communications all but gone. This performance has been extremely disappointing, with results weaker than I expected. I last covered shares in May, rating CHTR a Buy. Since then, the stock has lost a quarter of its value, requiring a re-analysis of the thesis.

In the company’s second quarter, Charter earned $9.18, which missed estimates badly, by $0.48, even as revenue of $13.8 billion was in-line with expectations. Residential revenue fell by 0.4% to $10.7 billion while commercial revenue grew by 0.8% to $1.8 billion as CHTR has made inroads with small business customers. Expenses grew by 0.6% to $8.1 billion, causing modest EBITDA margin compression with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 41.4%. As a result, adjusted EBITDA of $5.7 billion was up by just 0.5% from last year. I expected expenses to be closer to flat, which drove the EPS shortfall.

There is such profound fear around the cable company complex about these businesses facing secular decline, amidst changing consumer behavior and rising competition that even modest misses can cause large moves in shares. Clearly, this is not a growth business, as evidenced by the fact while passings were up 2.5% from last year to 57.5 million, its customer count is down 2% from last year to 31.2 million, pointing to ongoing market share challenges.

My view has been that Charter’s business is a melting ice cube: facing ongoing but manageable subscriber losses, rather than one facing sharp decline. So long as customer losses are less than 2% per year, the company should be able to offset that with pricing actions to maintain stable free cash flow. Indeed, its monthly residential bill is $123, up 1.7% from last year. The question is to what extent this quarter undermines this thesis. Frankly, while there were clear areas of disappointment, I believe the market’s reaction is significantly disproportionate to the reality.

Now, at $6 billion, Internet is the primary driver of revenue, and it is Charter’s most important business line. While consumers are able to cut cable TV access, if anything, the need for internet is never greater than now. The pool of internet users should continue to rise, though cable companies’ share of that pool faces pressure from nontraditional competitors.

Internet subscribers fell by 117k to 29.9 million. This was worse than expected and has caused concern about a steepening pace of decline. Internet performance has been increasingly choppy, and the better Q1 result raised hopes we may be seeing a turnaround, which were dashed in Q2. 2024 results were negatively impact by the expiration of government subsidies. For instance, in Q2, net adds were weighed down by the loss of 50k customers as subsidies expired. Excluding this one time impact, internet losses were 18k wore than last year.

Clearly, increased competition from wireless providers and even SpaceX has weighed on cable companies’ broadband results, even as these new technologies have relatively small penetration. Now, despite a 1.5% lower subscriber count, revenue was up 2.8% from last year, given the end of promotional pricing packages and typical bill inflation. Whereas I previously hoped to see internet subscriber losses shrink towards 1% per year, there is increasing risk of 1.5-2% annual pressure. Still, even with this pace of decline, internet revenue should continue to rise.

Now, the decline in video has been a long-term phenomenon. This product is lower margin to begin with given the cost of programming, so declines here have been less painful. There is actually some sign that the pace of losses may be improving, given management actions. Residential video losses were 73k, and video losses were actually the best in over a year. It does appear that the inclusion of several streaming services, like ad-supported Disney+ and Paramount+, has helped to stem the losses of video subscribers. Nonetheless, subscribers are down 5% from last year to 12.6 million. Now unlike internet, where revenue is outpacing subscribers, the opposite is true in video.

Video revenue declines were 9.9% to $3.5 billion. Customers are moving to smaller, lower-cost packages, reducing revenue, however this lost revenue is largely offset by lower programming costs. From an accounting perspective, its streaming package costs of $67 million are also netted against revenue rather than listed as expense. Essentially, these deals are helping CHTR to retain the customer, but it is sharing more of their economics with the streaming service provider.

The one growth engine for the company has been its mobile offering. Mobile growth was 500,000 to 10.9 million lines. Revenue growth remains strong, up 25%, slightly outpacing subscriber growth. However, mobile revenue remains relatively small at $921 million. Still as you can see, mobile growth is steadily decelerating. With over 10 million lines, CHTR has likely converted the majority of its customer base that wants a mobile relationship, which will likely continue to slow mobile growth towards ~15% by the end of next year, still a healthy pace.

Given these dynamics, we continue to see attrition in the share of customers who have 3 products to only having 2 products, largely reflecting customers who drop their cable package, while maintaining a mobile and Internet connection. 58% of customers no longer have a video relationship, and I expect this share to grow, albeit at a slowing rate.

One other headwind aside from its traditional business was that advertising sales were also down 7% to $371 million. Its local news stations benefited from political advertising, which hits its peak in Presidential election years, and we will likely see worsening declines in Q3 and Q4, given this seasonal, unfavorable comparison. Excluding political spending, ad revenue was still down by 4.4% from last year, worse than ~ 2% drop I expected.

Even with this pressure, Charter is a very cash generative business. In Q2, it produced $1 billion of free cash flow. This was down $250 million from last year, primarily due to the timing of tax payments and working capital movements. Cap-ex was essentially flat from last year at $2.9 billion. this spending should moderate over the next three years as it completes its network improvement and buildout project.

With cash flow, CHTR remains aggressive in returning capital to investors. It did $1.7 billion of buybacks during the quarter, or about 1.4% of the share count. There has been 2.2% share count reduction over the past year. I expect the pace of share count reduction to increase as blackouts have passed. With the slower pace of buybacks during blackouts last year, CHTR now has 4.18x debt/EBITDA leverage, down from 4.3x last year. The company carries $94 billion of debt. I expect the company to maintain leverage in this area until the Cox deal closes, likely in 2027, meaning we can expect it to return essentially all free cash flow to investors.

Back in May, I felt CHTR has ~$36 of standalone free cash flow once cap-ex reaches its normalized $9 billion level. Cox should add $2-4 of free cash flow per share. Now assuming a 0.5% worse annual performance in internet net adds, I still see CHTR with $34/share of underlying free cash flow at $9 billion of cap-ex. If the stock stays this low, more share count reduction from the buyback may actually close this $2 gap. That level will be closer to $18 this year, given higher cap-ex spending.

At $311, shares have an ~11% standalone run-rate free cash flow yield, which is really pricing in an ongoing decline in free cash flow. I have previously been using an ~8% free cash flow yield to value CHTR, given my view the company should generate stable to modest free cash flow growth over time. In this environment of fear around the sector, that multiple may be too aggressive, even as I continue to view it as fundamentally reasonable.

Still, I believe CHTR should trade no less than 10x normalized free cash flow. Even with the low-end of Cox synergies, that is $360 and would still support a 10% long-term return even if CHTR cannot raise prices faster than it loses customers. I view this as a conservative assumption, but the market may not be willing to assume much more than this until proven otherwise.

This is dramatically below my $465 prior fair value, reflecting a much lower free cash flow multiple, but I simply struggle to see much multiple expansion given sentiment. Ultimately, while I don't think "fair value" has fallen that much, my price target of what to expect investors will value at CHTR has fallen as fear offsets fundamental cheapness.

Nonetheless, that points to 16% upside under a very conservative assumption, which makes clear just how extreme the market reaction has been. At this point, I believe valuation is becoming very disconnected from fundamentals, and this is a time for patient investors to be buying into the decline. As such, I am moving shares up to a Strong Buy. This selloff is overdone, and with share buybacks ramping up, CHTR will, in my view, recover losses over time and deliver a strong outcome for investors willing to buy into the fear.

data.title: China's Economy Shows Its Resilience Amid Tariff Threats, But Headwinds Remain
data.description:
Recent data show’s China’s economy outperforming expectations. But the world’s second-largest economy continues to face challenges, including deflationary pressures and uneven employment levels. Haining Zha, Vice President and Director, Asset Allocation Research at TD Asset Management, discusses the outlook for the economy and what that could mean for Chinese markets.

Kim Parlee: We are here with Haining Zha, about to delve into what's happening with China.

I want to start with maybe just the highlights. I mean, if you look at the latest metrics coming out of China, I mean, I think the GDP is moving pretty quickly-- the headline. I think, if I look at the markets, I mean, Shanghai is up 9%, Hang Seng has been doing pretty well. Things look really good. Are they?

Haining Zha: I would say there are some pull-forward effect that makes people still worry about the second half of this year. For example, take export. First of all, US and China deal with this trade war really, really well. So it's kind of counterintuitive. If there is one country that dealt with it particularly well, that is China, and they are adversary-- being treated as adversary.

Kim Parlee: I think everyone is being treated as an adversary. But anyway.

Haining Zha: Yeah. And looking at the first half of this year, when you're looking at the, for example, export growth, the overall Chinese export growth is still growing at 7% year-over-year. And the only country that is seeing the decline is-- the only destination seeing decline is basically US, down 10%. All of the other region is growing. One of its biggest partner, ASEAN countries-- as a bloc-- they are growing at 10% year-over-year. Export to Europe growing roughly 4% year-over-year. Even Japan and Korea are seeing some moderate growth.

And also, if you're looking by product, the machinery and electronics, right now, they are already 60% of the overall export. It is growing at 10%. So, within the export sector, it is also having the structural change to move higher to the value chain. So overall, I would say in terms of export, China is dealing it pretty well.

Now, going to the consumption--

Kim Parlee: Yeah, domestic.

Haining Zha: Domestic consumption, last year it was very soft, but this year benefited from the government subsidy and trading program. So far, it has been much better than last year. But the problem is some of the subsidy will be depleted soon, which trigger people's worry about the sustainability in the second half.

Kim Parlee: What about-- I know deflation's on your radar too. And is that something that you're watching in terms of where the economy is going?

Haining Zha: Yeah, deflation is certainly a big topic. So if you look at the China CPI, it is hovering around zero. If you're looking at PPI, it is still negative. So, China is certainly experiencing that deflationary cycle right now.

But on the other hand, that's exactly the reason why the monetary policy has been very supportive. The fiscal policy is incrementally even more supportive. And I think the only thing we worry about is, in terms of monetary policy, people probably shouldn't expect to be-- don't expect huge stimulus, because the rate, the overall interest rate, has already been low. If they further decrease the interest rate, then they basically will hurt the net interest margin of the banking system. And as we see from the European banking experience, that won't be good.

And I think they could later on go onto unconventional paths and have QE and all that. But that really needs systemic shock for that to happen.

Kim Parlee: How is this all going to play out in the markets? I mentioned at the beginning that the Shanghai Composite has almost double digits this year, not quite. Hang Seng has done well. How is this going to translate in terms of Chinese markets and equities going forward?

Haining Zha: Yeah, that's the thing I want to talk about. So, when we hear things about Chinese economy, you have the impression that they are mired in deflationary cycle. So for sure, for the Chinese equity market-- it should be in bear market, right? But what I suspect is we are secretively going into a bull market. It is kind of go under the radar, unnoticed at this moment.

But there are two metrics that I want to mention. Number one is the performance. So if you look at a year-to-date performance, MSCI China or the Hong Kong Hang Seng market is basically generating 20% plus year-to-date return-- outperform all of the other regions. US are roughly 10%. Europe, also 20% in US dollar terms. But if you look in local currency in euro terms, half of that is coming from the currency gain. So if you're looking at local currency, it is also only around 10%. And Japan is basically flat in local currency terms. So it really outperformed.

My second metric is the IPO activity. So this year, Hong Kong IPO market has experienced a revival. So year-to-date, the IPO volume is around 107 billion Hong Kong dollars. So in US dollar terms, that's about $15 billion. So US IPO volume is still higher, sitting around-- the first half of this year-- around $27 billion. But if you look into the mix, about $11-12 billion is coming from SPACs, which is Special Purpose Acquisition Vehicles, not operating companies. So if you remove that, that is actually on par with the Hong Kong market. And we know the US capital market is a magnet of the global capital flow. So for Hong Kong to match that, that's kind of unusual.

Kim Parlee: What's-- I've only got about 45 seconds, so I apologize. What's driving this secret bull market, and will it continue?

Haining Zha: Well, there are multiple drivers. For example, people seeing the AI innovation, the semiconductor breakthrough.

Kim Parlee: It's what's driving the US markets too, at the same time.

Haining Zha: Driving the US market too. But the Chinese people, Chinese market people don't expect innovation to come out of that space. But when they see it, then all of a sudden they want to re-rate that market.

Original Post

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities persevered through tariff and geopolitical scares to deliver solid gains in the second quarter. The broad market S&P 500 Index returned 10.9%, swinging from a near-bear market to all-time high. The benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index did even better as risk-on sentiment took hold, rising 17.8%, and outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index by over 1,400 basis points.
  • Contributors: Holdings in Netflix (NFLX), Eaton (ETN) and Intuit (INTU) as well as underweights to Apple (AAPL) and Eli Lilly (LLY). Stock selection in the communication services and industrials; an underweight to consumer staples.
  • Detractors: Holdings in UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and Marsh & McLennan (MMC), as well as an underweight to Broadcom. Stock selection in health care and information technology (IT); an overweight to health care and underweight to IT.
  • Outlook: While the S&P 500 has broadened, the Russell 1000 Growth Index remains dominated by its eight $1 trillion+ market cap components. We remain confident and committed to our more balanced, risk-aware approach to growth investing, seeking to add value through differentiated stock selection.

Performance Review

  • The Fund’s more diversified and defensive exposure compared to our concentrated benchmark reversed from a tailwind in the first quarter to a headwind in the second. We were disappointed with the weak quarterly performance of several of our more countercyclical holdings. UnitedHealth Group saw a renewed selloff following a first-quarter earnings miss and guidance reduction as the managed care company cited elevated cost pressure and the exit of its CEO.
  • Thermo Fisher Scientific lagged due to concerns around spending cuts in the academic/government and biopharma segments due to regulatory fears and tariffs. Insurance broker Marsh & McLennan fell due to the unwind of the flight to quality trade along with concern around a softer insurance pricing environment.
  • Mega cap positioning was mostly neutral as an overweight to Meta (META) and underweight to underperforming Apple offset relative headwinds from underweights to Microsoft and new addition Broadcom (AVGO).
  • Netflix in communication services, one of the Fund’s largest active weights, saw its shares rise due to overall continued robust execution with double-digit revenue growth, driven by a balance of subscriber growth and price, and continued margin expansion. We remain confident in the company’s long-term strategy, strong market position and attractiveness of the global streaming market.
  • Industrials also did well, buoyed by stocks tied to power and electrification, like out-of-benchmark portfolio holding Eaton.
  • In IT, we trimmed Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) to manage the risk amid intensified geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China, directing the proceeds into purchases of AI-indexed chip makers Broadcom and Marvell Technology (MRVL) and software maker ServiceNow (NOW).
  • The additions of commercial airline manufacturer Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and industrials gases provider Linde (LIN) helped diversify the portfolio beyond traditional growth areas and prepare for wider large-cap leadership.
  • Our sales targeted companies whose end markets have become further challenged due to the broader macro backdrop – mass market retailer Target (TGT) and contract research organization ICON (ICLR) — or technological challenges in the AI era – software maker Adobe.

Outlook

  • Boosted by AI enthusiasm along with an easing of volatility and a renewed embrace of risk taking, IT finished the second quarter at 49% of the Russell 1000 Growth Index (RLG), the second-highest concentration since parts of the sector were reclassified in 2018 and 2023. While the S&P 500 Index has shown signs of broadening as judged by the advance/decline line and the number of stocks closing above their 50-day moving average, the RLG remains dominated by its eight $1 trillion+ market cap components.
  • Our longtime IT underweight specifically, and our sector allocations more generally, are a function of managing concentration and industry risk and striving to maintain a portfolio with a diversified set of growth drivers. To this end, the long-term success of the Fund will always be guided by fundamental stock selection. The second quarter is a reminder that there will be periods and market conditions when the Fund will be challenged in keeping pace with the benchmark. We remain confident and committed to our more balanced, risk-aware approach to growth investing, seeking to add value through sector and stock selection investing in differentiated, well-positioned companies.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2026 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Short selling is a speculative strategy. Unlike the possible loss on a security that is purchased, there is no limit on the amount of loss on an appreciating security that is sold short. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. Diversification does not guarantee a pro1t or protect against a loss. Active management does not ensure gains or protect against market declines. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identi1ed or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. Source: FTSE. Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

ClearBridge Investments, LLC, and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

data.title: Brilliant Bargains: 3 Opportunistic Stocks Under $10
data.description:
Trump And The Art Of ‘Perhaps The Largest Deal Ever Made’

Investors breathed a sigh of relief this week as news of multiple 15% trade deals fueled a market rebound and U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent dispelled fears over instability at the helm of the Federal Reserve. As volatility begins to subside, the spotlight is back on interest rates.

On Tuesday, U.S. President Donald Trump announced a “massive” trade deal with Japan. The arrangement would involve placing a 15% reciprocal tariff on the country’s U.S. exports and a reported cut in auto duties, “perhaps the largest Deal ever made.” Separately, the U.S. and the European Union are reportedly close to striking a trade deal that could impose a 15% tariff on European imports.

U.S. equities rallied due to optimism about the trade deal. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq notched record highs on Wednesday, climbing 0.78% and 0.61%, respectively. Gains were led by power equipment maker GE Vernova (GEV), which surged 14.6% an all-time high, and Nvidia (NVDA), which climbed 2.5%. On Thursday, the S&P another 0.3%, while the Nasdaq fell 0.2%.

Bessent put investor anxieties to rest this week that Fed Chair Jerome Powell would need to resign, shortly after Trump acknowledged that he is no longer aiming to fire Powell, stating: “He’s going to be out pretty soon anyway.” Treasury yields inched up higher on Wednesday as a result: the benchmark ten-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was last trading more than four basis points higher at 4.39%, while the five-year yield rose more than five basis points to 3.9% and the two-year yield rose 4.5 basis points to 3.9%. (Find the live chart on Seeking Alpha’s Market Data page.)

Treasury Yields Inch Higher (as of 7/24/25)

Interest Rates Reenter The Spotlight As Volatility Cools

Meanwhile, the CBOE Volatility Index, often called Wall Street’s fear gauge, fell more than 6.8% on Wednesday to a five-month low of 15.37 as market jitters continued to subside.

Wall Street’s ‘Fear Gauge’ Hit Five-Month Low on Wednesday (7/23/25)

The spotlight now turns to interest rates, as volatility cools and Trump doubles down on calls for the U.S. central bank to make cuts. "With a striking of the pen," the president said, the Fed can save the federal government at least $1T in interest rate payments. "Those rates should be three points lower, maybe more than that."

Trump may not get his wish just yet. According to the CME FedWatch tool, trading activity indicates a 97.4% probability that rates will stay unchanged at the Fed's current benchmark policy rate of 4.25%-4.50% at the July meeting.

Fed Futures Traders Are Betting Heavily That Interest Rates Won’t Change

Why Value Stocks Shine In Times Of Market Recovery

As markets rebound, investors often shift from safe-haven assets into more undervalued areas. Value stocks usually perform well during economic recoveries or early-stage bull markets as investors embrace more risk, leading to higher earnings growth and multiple expansion for undervalued companies with strong fundamentals.

At the same time, it is critical to maintain a flexible and resilient portfolio as uncertainty remains over whether the Fed will follow Trump’s wishes and lower interest rates. Striking a balance between value stocks (which could continue to benefit if rates remain unchanged) and growth stocks (which could thrive if rates are slashed) can help prepare your portfolio for whatever comes next.

Using SA’s Quant system, I selected three stocks under $10 to seize upon the current market rebound and growing trade optimism. While I normally avoid low-priced stocks—often the cheapest and some of the most volatile assets in the stock market—the stocks discussed below represent a blend of attractive valuations, above-average growth, and strong balance sheets.

My proprietary method combines powerful computer processing with what I like to call ‘Quantamental’ analysis, designed to remove emotion from the investment process. The SA algorithm separates the weak companies from the strong based on their historical financials, analysts' consensus growth estimates, and hundreds of financial metrics. (Curious how our Quant Factor Grades work? Learn more here.)

In addition to being fundamentally strong, my top three opportunistic stocks under $10 offer high return potential at lower entry points. Let’s dive in.

Seize The Moment: 3 Opportunistic Stocks Under $10

FinVolution Group (NYSE: FINV)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.49B

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 5 out of 690

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 2 out of 38

A member of the PRO Quant Portfolio and one of my Top 10 Stocks For H2 2025, FinVolution Group (FINV) is a consumer lending platform that connects borrowers and businesses across China and other parts of Asia.

FINV is in a strong position following excellent Q1 2025 results. Revenues increased by 9.4% year-over-year, while transaction volume rose 7.9% Y/Y. Its upward climb has benefited from strong growth tailwinds, including China’s push to boost domestic lenders as the government looks to ease regulation in the credit sector.

As SA analyst Bay Area Ideas recently wrote:

FinVolution is showing strong operational metrics, with robust user growth in both China and international markets, indicating rapid adoption and resilience…The stock trades at a deep discount to sector peers, making it undervalued; I am initiating FinVolution at a buy rating based on these strengths.

FINV Stock’s Quant Breakdown

FINV is an incredible bargain, as evidenced by its ‘A+’ valuation grade. Its forward GAAP P/E of 5.95 is nearly a 51% discount to the sector, while its forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 2.68 is over a 75% discount to the 10.83 sector median.

The company also earns an ‘A+’ in momentum, with one-year price performance of 94.7%, an 808.1% difference from the 10.4% sector median. Growth has steadily improved over the past three months from a ‘C+’ to a ‘B’, boosted higher by a forward free cash flow per share growth rate of 61%, a 371% difference from the 13% sector median.

Profitability is also strong, with a return on total assets TTM of 10.65%, an 840.3% difference to the 1.1% sector median. FINV has also received four positive EPS revisions from analysts over the past 90 days, and zero negative EPS revisions.

Next up is another cutting-edge Chinese fintech group offering the perfect balance of value and growth.

LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (NASDAQ: LX)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.25B

  • Sector: Finance

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 2 out of 690

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 1 out of 38

Ranked third among all financials and first among consumer finance stocks, LexinFintech Holdings Ltd. (LX) is a Chinese consumer finance platform and e-commerce business. The company offers customers personal loans to purchase items on its e-commerce site Fenquile, which carries everything from electronics to clothing to household essentials.

After losing more than 30% during the height of the March/April global sell-off following Trump’s ‘Liberation Day’ tariffs, the Shenzhen-based fintech has bounced back to return 30% YTD thanks to its strong Quant fundamentals and healthy balance sheet.

Highlights from its Q1 2025 results included adjusted net income attributable to ordinary shareholders of RMB472M ($65.7M), a Y/Y increase of more than 100%, and a 19% quarter-over-quarter and 113% year-over-year increase in net income to RMB430M ($60M). The group also announced a share repurchase program this week to buy back “no more than US$50 million worth of its own shares” over the next 12 months

LX Stock’s Quant Breakdown

I predicted LX’s resurgence months ago, but the broader market has yet to catch on to this hidden gem. Despite its strong growth outlook, its share price remains incredibly cheap ($7.55 as of 7/24/25). Fueling its impressive valuation grade is an ‘A+’ forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 4.09, a 63.6% discount from the 11.24 sector median. A forward price-to-sales ratio of 0.64, a 77.5% discount from the sector, emphasizes the low price of its shares relative to its potential sales growth as it continues to expand its lending business.

As SA analyst Chris Bazzle recently observed:

LexinFintech Holdings Ltd.'s pivot to a capital-light, tech-empowerment model reduces risk, boosts margins, and aligns with Chinese regulatory preferences, driving future profitability…Dividend growth, robust balance sheet, and sector-beating momentum reinforce my Buy rating for LX as a compelling deep-value opportunity.

LX earns an ‘A+’ in momentum, with nine-month price performance of 131.9%—a 1,813% difference to the 6.9% sector median. Growth also earns an ‘A+’ with forward EBITDA growth of 34.4%, a 185% difference from the 12% sector median, and forward EPS GAAP growth of 42.3%, a 313.2% difference.

While FINV and LX have made strides in fintech, my next selection is a trailblazer in artificial intelligence.

CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (NASDAQ: COMM)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.78B

  • Sector: Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 11 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 7/24/2025): 1 out of 39

A top-performing stock in SA’s PRO Quant Portfolio, CommScope (COMM) is leveraging explosive demand for artificial intelligence through its data center infrastructure solutions for hyperscalers, among other tech infrastructure services. The Claremont, North Carolina-based group has returned more than 56% YTD and a stunning 352.7% over the past 12 months.

The group reported strong Q1 results, including a +23% Y/Y increase in revenue to $1.11B (+23.3% Y/Y). Its enterprise fiber business grew 88% Y/Y to $213M, profiting off explosive data center demand this year.

Deutsche Bank recently added the stock to its Catalyst Call Buy list, writing:

We believe business fundamentals remain strong across each of COMM's segments, with recent data points reaffirming this view. Collectively, we believe COMM is likely to trend towards the upper end of its 2025E outlook for Core Adj. EBITDA (DBe: $1.05B, vs. guidance for $1.00-1.05B).

COMM Stock’s Quant Breakdown

I highlighted COMM in May as a top choice for value investors due to its ‘A+’ valuation grade and high growth potential. That assessment still stands: the stock’s forward GAAP P/E of 6.74 is more than a 78% discount from the 31.19 sector median. Its forward non-GAAP PEG of 0.22 is over an 88% discount to the 1.88 sector median.

COMM also scores an A+ in momentum, with three-month price performance of nearly 117%, a 342% difference from the 26.4% sector median. Growth is also impressive, with long-term forward EPS growth of 42.6%, a 198.8% difference from the 14.25 sector median.

With six upward EPS revisions and one negative EPS revision over the past 90 days, analysts appear in line with SA Quant’s Strong Buy rating.

Conclusion: Big Bargains, Big Potential Gains

Like FINV and LX, COMM’s opportunistic balance of value and growth makes it a great option in an improving macroeconomic environment. Maintaining a mixture of both investment styles can help you capture the market's upside while remaining adaptable and resilient should more volatility arise.

For additional options, SA Quant’s custom screening tool can help you screen for more Buy and Strong-Buy picks from various sectors, market capitalizations, Quant Factor grades, and more.

PRO tip: Seeking Alpha's new PRO Quant Portfolio has returned 19.47% vs. the S&P’s 6.04% (as of 7/24/25) since launching last month. Watch my recent webinar to learn how to leverage the actively managed, data-driven model, which offers a blend of 30 stocks spanning various geographies and market capitalizations.

Alternatively, consider joining our Alpha Picks community to receive two new Quant Strong Buy portfolio additions monthly, with real-time sell alerts when ratings shift and detailed performance analysis.

data.title: American Airlines: Consumer Bifurcation Weakens Economy Class Travel In Q2
data.description:
Pressure continues to mount on airline stocks. American Airlines (NASDAQ:AAL) crashed over 9% on Thursday after announcing Q2 earnings. It beat on revenue with 0.4% annual sales growth and an adjusted EPS of $0.95, beating targets by $0.18. However, its guidance downshifted, looking to lower booked revenues and a weaker demand trend, implying negative profits in Q3 unless booking trends improve.

Its Q2 performance showed a decent recovery in travel demand, with premium sales rebounding; however, its labor costs grew disproppateiony, creating margin pressures. Operationally, its passenger revenue per mile seat fell 3.6% from Q2 2024, and total revenue per mile seat was 2.7% lower. Non-GAAP operating costs per available seat mile rose 3.4% annually. Combined with a weaker sales outlook for Q3, it seems American may be facing a dreaded decline in both sales and margins. Most airline stocks fell in response, with the (JETS) ETF dropping 4.4%.

I covered American Airlines last in the March article, "American Airlines: Industry Profits Will Fall Until Consolidation Limits Competition." My view was bearish, but with a long-term focus on its competitive and macro headwinds. At the time, the stock was in a sharp crash triggered by the tariff-driven market correction. I noted that, although its share price had fallen around 40%, its so indebted that its enterprise value was only around 11% lower, making it not considerably discounted for higher demand risks. Further, I expected that major airlines like American would struggle with sustaining higher margins until the industry consolidates. Since then, AAL is roughly flat, 3.6% higher, a small gain for its volatility level, while the S&P 500 is up 12%.

In my view, American Airlines' Q2 performance validates this outlook. That said, I focused primarily on the long-term negative trend in most airlines' operating margins. Given its weaker guidance, a focus on short-term technical and macro demand factors may be more important. For me, the key question today is whether or not air travel demand will remain stable or weaken, as that, combined with the long-term cost pressure trend, threatens to dramatically lower AAL's profit outlook.

Market Bifurcation Lowers Domestic Demand

American Airlines' Q2 investor call highlighted consumer bifurcation demand trends, with lower overall demand and comparative sales, offset by strength in international and premium travel. First class is growing while economy class is shrinking. Its rewards/loyalty program is faring decently as upper-income groups see higher travel demand, sustaining the "pent-up demand" post-COVID rebound. However, it's seeing a material drop in domestic market demand, accounting for 70% of its revenue.

In my view, this fundamentally relates to the long-term trend toward consumer bifurcation. I've noted this trend in numerous recent articles, seeing it as the key factor for understanding the current economic environment. Although the "shrinking middle class" shift has been in place for decades, I argue it has accelerated since 2023 as inflation and rates have had a greater impact. Since then, we've seen a rise in average inflation-adjusted income, combined with a decline in median inflation-adjusted income. See below:

Notably, median family/household income has still risen since 2023; however, this is likely due to a sharp rise in multiple jobholders. In my view, American Airlines is hampered by this change, though it is probably most bearish for airlines like Southwest (LUV) that focus most squarely on the middle-income market. American Airlines has some capacity to offset economy-class pressures by continuing to grow its first-class and loyalty programs, which should remain strong as long as average incomes rise faster than the median (i.e, wider income distributions).

That said, I argue that we're seeing recession-like pressures in American Airlines' "bread and butter," its domestic economy-class traveler. We're seeing this with the bankruptcy of Spirit Airlines and the general weakness in Frontier (ULCC) and JetBlue (JBLU). Conversely, Delta (DAL) is almost entirely unphased:

In 2025, two-year-ahead sales expectations for low-cost carriers JetBlue and Frontier have declined, with the outlook for American Airlines declining more recently (this data is not yet updated for the Thursday guidance cut). Delta, which is not known for discounts and has a great international/premium travel and loyalty program, is basically immune to this strain. American seems to fare in the middle, similar to Southwest.

Air passenger transportation demand is correlated to employment fundamentals. The overall US unemployment rate rose a bit last year, but has stabilized at a healthy level, lowering recession concerns. However, again, the overall average is not necessarily representative. For example, we're seeing a sustained rise in those unemployed for a year or more, still looking for work. We're also seeing a very low hiring rate. Combined with trends of lower median wages and greater multiple job holders, I think this clearly points to economic strain for a large portion of the middle-class, explaining why transportation demand is stagnating and a lower normal (from the pre-2020 "normal"). See below:

From the lower chart, we can see that this is occurring as airline fare prices decline compared to consumer prices in general. On the one hand, it is alarming that passenger transportation demand is weak despite lower real airline fares. On the other hand, airline fares may be compressed due to increased price competition for a shrinking/stagnating demand pool. Of course, the primary driver of ticket prices is fuel costs, which have declined significantly since 2022.

In my view, oil and fuel prices will most likely rebound over the coming years due to today's very low CapEx investments in the oil extraction/refinery industries, limiting the supply outlook. Should oil or fuel prices spike due to weaker production or geopolitical factors, I expect economic demand pressure on airlines will rise significantly, giving demand is weakening despite relative affordability.

American Airlines is in Poor Financial Health

Its managers also noted one-off costs from disruptive operational activities (storms, etc.), which seems to be a growing issue for airlines. Further, its nonfuel cost outlook has risen to 4.5% due to union agreements. This follows the trend we've seen since COVID, with acceleration in wages for pilots and flight attendants due to higher labor turnover and a (now fading) travel surge.

Since the market shifted around 2023, AAL's revenue and expenses have plateaued. Its CapEx budget remains cut, though it still consumes much of its profits, leaving the company with very slim free cash flows:

If TTM revenues slip more, as is virtually certain in Q3, American Airlines may face chronically negative free cash flows. The company's balance sheet is not healthy enough to sustain a chronically negative cash flow. Its non-current long-term debt is around $25.2B, with around $4.6B in current long-term debt. Its immediate liquidity is decent with $8.5B in cash and short-term investments, but its working capital is around -$10B, with far more current liabilities than assets. Its working capital improved from Q1 (shown in the chart below), and is generally negative, given that airlines have naturally high unearned revenues. However, the fact that its working capital trend is negative and far greater than its market capitalization is alarming:

In my opinion, as I've mentioned in previous articles, I do not think AAL would go through a larger recession without significant financial impairment. It has become so leveraged over the past decade (largely due to COVID loans) that its equity is very small compared to the overall size of the company. Thus, we should not be surprised to see AAL swing 50% every year.

Imagine drawing a line near the top of a glass of water, then shaking the glass - it would not take a big shake for the water level to fall below the drawn line. That analogy may seem silly, but I see online comments from those bullish on AAL stating (along the lines of), "the company is trading at a 50% to 70% discount from before COVID," overlooking the fact that all of its market cap declines are accounted for by increased debt. Its enterprise value, which accounts for debt and cash, is around 10-15% lower, but I argue that is reasonable given airline demand fundamentals never fully recovered, have stagnated, and may now be in decline.

Still, I expect cost pressures will slow. For one, airlines do not face employee shortages that drive wage pressure and collective bargaining power. Data from last year on pilot training showed a huge increase in activity, potentially pushing the pilot market into a surplus, particularly with lower demand. Union deals are still pushing market wages up in some employee segments, but in general, I expect costs to grow similar or slower than current guidance by 2027, mainly due to lower hiring.

There are also new cost-saving factors for airlines, such as using AI to automate customer service work and improve ticket pricing. Interestingly, American Airlines' CEO Robert Isom is critical of this, seeing AI pricing as inappropriate. US senators argue that Delta uses AI to set ticket prices individually, creating pricing based on one's data, theoretically pushing prices up to one's "pain point".

This is called "surveillance pricing," wherein an airline using such tools may set prices based on reviewing one's browsing history as a means of estimating a person's maximum price, meaning two people may pay different prices for the same ticket. Delta's President called the AI tool a "super analyst," noting "amazingly favorable unit revenues," also noting a goal to gain a first-mover advantage in AI pricing.

This is a tricky issue, as avoiding technological developments may mean American Airlines misses out on potential tools that may lower operating costs and increase revenues. However, I tend to agree with Isom, as the risk of regulatory and consumer backlash is too great.

The Bottom Line

I remain bearish on AAL, and expect the stock to decline in value through the end of the year as investors and analysts brace for a sustained decline in demand. Although we're seeing strength in the upper-tier section of AAL's business, I would be surprised if high-income Americans avoid economic pressures. Arguably, higher-salary jobs are more exposed to AI demand pressures, though this is still a mostly hypothetical issue. For now, this trend is isolated to entry-level post-college jobs, potentially cascading upward as such tools are improved.

Compared to most analysts, my cost outlook for AAL is likely more bullish than many, expecting operating cost growth to reverse as the labor pool improves. However, my outlook for demand is very bearish. Even if AAL's sales decline 4-5% due to economic weakness, that would likely give it negative cash flows, jeopardizing its financial stability, given its weak balance sheet. AAL's market capitalization is also pretty low compared to its debt and liquidity needs; if it raised capital through equity sales, it would be very dilutive for investors.

Finally, I do not think these economic trends are fundamentally cyclical. It is one thing for American Airlines to have losses for a year due to economic weakness; it is another if it is three or more years. At this point, my view is very speculative, but given economic bifurcation trends (harming economy-tier sales and pricing) are accelerating after existing for a few decades, I suspect AAL may face demand pressures through 2030. Travel activity has slipped despite decent "average" economic trends and lower real ticket prices, so a true recession and potentially higher ticket prices (due to fuel costs) could be more catastrophic.

Still, I would not short AAL. Short interest in the stock is relatively high at roughly 11%. Although its significant leverage makes it, to me, overvalued, fundamental improvement would cause a large increase in its share price. Last year, an outlook rebound caused the stock to nearly double. Although I do not expect this will occur soon, eventually, I think airline capacity will fall (through liquidations, etc.), leading to more industry consolidation that reduces today's extreme price competition.

Arguably, AAL also has a strong "implied put" given the government's historical willingness to bailout airlines, particularly the Big Three. In my opinion, AAL would not have naturally survived 2020 if not for this relief program. That was justified because airlines faced losses due to government lockdown rules. However, the fact remains that American Airlines does not exactly operate in a free market, creating huge risks for short-sellers.

data.title: Cytokinetics Merits A Speculative Buy Rating
data.description:
Today, I put Cytokinetics, Incorporated (NASDAQ:CYTK) in the spotlight for the first time in 2025. I last posted research on this mid-cap biopharma in November 2024. The stock hit a bump when a snag occurred around the approval process of its lead drug. However, the shares have started to rebound and have moved higher over the past couple of weeks. The start of a longer-term rally or just a 'head fake'? An updated analysis follows in the paragraphs below.

Cytokinetics, Incorporated is headquartered in San Francisco, CA. The company is focused on developing and commercializing muscle activators and inhibitors as potential treatments for debilitating diseases. The stock trades near $38 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $4.5 billion.

The company's primary drug candidate in its pipeline is called aficamten. This compound is designed to reduce excessive contractions in the heart, an affliction called hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or HCM. This is an affliction that affects approximately 300,000 Americans annually.

Management submitted a New Drug Application or NDA for aficamten on December 2, the original PDUFA date once the FDA accepted the application in December. In early May 2025, the FDA moved back its PDUFA date around this application from the late September to late December of this year.

The FDA cited the need for additional time to assess the data from the company's Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS), which was submitted after the NDA. It should be noted this is a technical issue. Not a single analyst firm downgraded the shares after this news, and it's quite common that more information is requested around a company's first NDA. A recent article on Seeking Alpha made the same point of the delay being logistical and not relating to rejection. It also noted the only other drug approved so far in this class, Camzyos, from Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY), went through a similar journey before being approved in the spring of 2022.

Management appears confident of eventual approval and the Cytokinetics' CEO stated:

We remain confident in the distinct benefit-risk and pharmaceutical profile of aficamten and continue to expect a differentiated label and risk mitigation profile upon its potential approval by the FDA

A week and a half after the FDA pushed back their decision data on aficamten by three months, the company posted positive trial results. This topline data was from a Phase 3 study evaluating aficamten to treat obstructive HCM. The 175-person study met its primary endpoint of statistically significant improvement in peak oxygen uptake.

It should be noted that marketing applications have been filed in Europe and in China with Cytokinetics' partner Sanofi (SNY). That agreement was signed in late 2024. Cytokinetics can garner up to $150 million in various milestone payments as well as royalties of low-to-high teens for commercialized sales of aficamten.

In addition, aficamten is being evaluated in other studies for other related indications. It should also be noted that Camzyos had a notable trial failure evaluating it to treat non-obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy earlier this year. Camzyos did $159 million of global sales in Q1, up nearly 70% year-over-year.

Cytokinetics also has several other candidates in its pipeline. It has another selective cardiac myosin activator called Omecamtive mercarbil. This candidate is being evaluated to treat patients that have heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. It's currently being evaluated in a three-year Phase 3 study.

Finally, we have a compound called CK-586, which is being evaluated to treat individuals with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) in earlier stage studies. It's currently enrolling a Phase 2 trial.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst community is almost universally positive on the prospects for Cytokinetics. Since first quarter results were posted, 14 analyst firms including RBC Capital, Barclays, Stifel Nicolaus and Morgan Stanley have reissued "Buy" ratings on the stock. Price targets range from $53 to $120 a share, with most price targets in the $60s to $80s. Bank of America maintained its "Hold" rating and $45 a share price target on the equity.

Approximately one out of eight shares of outstanding float is currently held short. Several insiders have been consistent sellers of the stock for years. Since first quarter number posted, insiders have disposed under $1.8 million in stock collectively.

After a net loss of $161.4 million for the quarter, Cytokinetics ended Q1 with approximately $1.1 billion of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. Management has guided that it expects GAAP operating expenses of $670 million to $710 million in FY2025. Management listed just under $650 million of long-term convertible debt and term loans on its 10-K filed for the quarter. Cytokinetics also has access to additional funding though other transactions (below).

Conclusion:

The stock is down just over 30% since Cytokinetics was last highlighted. The main driver of this decline was the three-month delay to the company's NDA for lead candidate aficamten. While the data was inconvenient as it pushed back potential revenues for aficamten by a quarter. However, delays in applications with the FDA is hardly unusual for the industry. The approval of aficamten will hopefully be a key inflection point for the company and lead to other potential launches in the following years.

The analyst community has maintained their faith in Cytokinetics and the company has multiple "shots on goal." The company is also well funded for the moment and has plenty of potential catalysts on the horizon. Aficamten is highly likely to be approved late this year. Hopefully it's the first of several approvals for this potential franchise drug. The current median analyst firm consensus sees Cytokinetics with over $2 billion in annual sales by 2030. (It also sees earnings of just over $5.50 a share that year).

Two last things. First, given its wholly owned pipeline, Cytokinetics makes a logical buyout candidate at some point. In late March, it was mentioned as a prime acquisition candidate here on Seeking Alpha. Finally, the options against this equity are both liquid and lucrative. Therefore, it makes a viable covered call candidate. And that's how I hold my position in this "Speculative Buy" within my portfolio.

data.title: Meta: Q2 Earnings Could Bring Another CapEx Increase
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) is retesting the all-time highs reached in February this year, in line with my previous expectations.

With Q2 earnings due next week (July 30th), I took the opportunity to revisit my bull case and list some of the key factors to watch in the earnings report.

First, another upward revision of CapEx is still on the table. Back in Q1, I noticed a bottleneck in Meta's computing capacity, which is likely affecting the ads and recommendation engines. Training and inferring demands from Llama are most likely limiting the computing power for serving ads to the users that are most likely to click them, despite a mid-single-digit increase in time spent on Meta's platforms attributed to their AI strategy. Overall, I wouldn't be surprised to see CapEx projections for 2025 coming in above the latest guidance of $64B - $72B.

Second, even though Reality Labs accounted for just 1.2% of total revenue in the past 12 months, I expect an improvement in growth driven by the partnership with Anduril and the physical stores strategy. I admit that this is a small segment; however, it burned more than $18B in the past 12 months. Therefore, any improvement (even in the topline) may be welcomed by the market.

Third, the advertising segment is largely benefiting from the improvements that Llama is bringing to the recommendation algorithms. In the 6 months preceding Q1 2025, this has led to a mid-to-high single-digit increase in time spent on Facebook and Instagram. Naturally, this has a positive effect on impressions, conversions, and ad spend by marketers.

From a risk perspective, the removal of the de minimis exemption in May is the main factor that makes me sweat. Also, I will keep a close eye on CapEx and whether the upward revision in Q1 has led to an increase in computing capacity from their previous baseline. The biggest concern is that the CapEx hike could be driven by rising supplier costs (in the likes of Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), or Intel (INTC)) from tariffs.

Overall, I believe Llama’s positive impact on ads significantly surpasses the headwinds from the end of de minimis. Furthermore, I believe tariffs on semis will land in the single digit, and reciprocal tariffs with Taiwan will be favorable, as President Trump’s last intention is to jeopardize the leading position of the U.S. in the AI race.

Q2 2025 Preview

CapEx will be one of the key metrics to keep an eye on in the upcoming earnings release. Back in Q1, Meta was the only of the big 4 hyperscalers that raised its FY 2025 CapEx guidance from a range of $60B–$65B to $64B–$72B.

At this point, I'd like to clarify why an upward CapEx revision would be hyper bullish for the stock. Back in Q1, CFO Susan Li made the following comment that, I admit, made me sweat:

Even with the capacity that we're bringing online in 2025, we are having a hard time meeting the demand that teams have for compute resources across the company.

Well, it turns out that this bottleneck is affecting both gen-AI (i.e., Llama) and core ad/recommendation algorithms. My main concern going into Q2 and beyond is a slowdown in ad revenue due to GPUs' capacity being prioritized for training and inferencing rather than serving ads to those who are most likely to click on them.

Another remark from Susan that made me sweat was regarding the driver to increase CapEx (emphasis added):

The higher cost we expect to incur for infrastructure hardware this year really comes from suppliers who source from countries around the world, and there's just a lot of uncertainty around this, given the ongoing trade discussions.

I have no doubts that analysts during the upcoming Q&A will ask the big question: has the CapEx upgrade in Q1 led to an increase in computing capacity compared to the baseline projections (i.e., the previous $60B–$65B range)?

If the answer is anything other than a clear "yes", then it is very likely that Meta is paying more for the same computing capacity that was planned at the start of the year.

Personally, I think the risk mentioned above is small. Why?

First, Alphabet (GOOG) has recently announced an upward CapEx revision of $10 billion above their previous expectation. The rationale behind this increase didn't include extra GPU costs due to tariffs (both reciprocal and sectoral). Instead, 2/3 of the incremental CapEx was for servers, and 1/3 was attributed to data centers and networking equipment. What I found remarkable is the fact that tariffs weren't even mentioned during Alphabet's earnings call.

Second, some market participants are sweating over the upcoming announcement of tariffs on semiconductors. With Trump's comments from back in February, some investors fear that tariffs will start at 25%.

Not me.

If it isn't clear by now, the Trump administration is using tariffs as leverage to get better deals with its trading partners around the world. It would make zero sense for Trump to hinder the U.S. dominance in the AI race just to make a few bucks (billions, that is) from tariffs. In the long term, the benefits of being first in the AI race are significantly larger than a 25% tax on semiconductors. Furthermore, Trump has already hinted at the tariff structure for semiconductors in his remarks about pharma (emphasis added):

We're going to start off with a low tariff and give the pharmaceutical companies a year or so to build, and then we're going to make it a very high tariff

I have a high conviction that tariffs on semis will come in at a value below 10% (possibly in the mid-single digit) and stay at that level indefinitely (not just a year).

Moving on, Threads will be in the spotlight in the upcoming earnings release as Meta introduced earlier this year ads on this platform in 30+ markets. That said, CFO Susan tempered the high expectations that the market has for this platform:

We don't expect Threads to be a meaningful driver of overall impression or revenue growth in 2025.

To me, at this early stage, revenue is not as important as the time spent by users on this platform. Back in Q1, CEO Mark reported a 35% increase in time spent on Threads, with more than 350 million MAUs. If I see a slowdown in these metrics due to the introduction of ads, I will begin to sweat.

Is this a big risk? Again, I don't think so. In the past quarter, the company attributed a 4% lift in time spent in Threads to Llama testing algorithms. I believe any deterioration in user experience due to ads will be easily compensated for by a better recommendation algorithm on this platform.

Finally, I considered including below Meta's latest guidance for Q2 and FY 2025:

I find it quite remarkable that the company managed to lower the guidance for expenses (from $114 billion - $119 billion) despite the fact that Meta was openly trying to poach top AI researchers from rivals like OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Apple, Anthropic, and Scale AI, with nine‑figure total compensation offers.

In regard to revenue, the Street is expecting revenue to land at a midpoint of $44.8B. I have no doubts the company will be able to easily meet (and perhaps exceed) this value. Why?

As I noted in my previous coverage, the Reality Labs segment is showing strong potential from the recent partnership with Anduril for a $100 million contract with the Pentagon. Furthermore, Meta opened in April their first physical store, located in Burlingame, California. This is hyperbullish for the Reality Labs segment, as it gives skeptics of the Meta Quest headsets and the RayBan AR glasses a chance to give them a try. You never know you need something until you try it.

On the risk side, the ads segment could be adversely affected by the removal of the de minimis import exemption earlier in May. In fact, CFO Susan already noted a drop in US ad spend from Asia-based e-commerce exporters.

Specifically, we have seen some reduced spending in the US from Asia-based e-commerce exporters, which we believe is in anticipation of the de minimis exemption going away on May 2nd

She further added:

It’s very early and hard to know how things will play out over the quarter, and certainly harder to know that for the rest of the year

So, it seems that there is some uncertainty around the final impact of these Asian advertisers on ad spend. I recommend keeping a close eye on this topic during the upcoming Q&A session, as analysts will likely ask for any updates.

Looking Ahead

I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on Meta going into Q2 earnings. I believe results will come (at the very least) in line with the Street's expectations for both revenue and EPS.

Looking at the top line, I expect Reality Labs to break the recent decline shown below and show an improvement driven by the partnership with Anduril and the recent strategy to open physical stores around the country.

Concerning the ad segment, I believe the increase in time spent on Meta's platforms due to the improvement of the recommendation algorithms will compensate for the revenue loss from the removal of the de minimis exemption. That said, this is a risk that readers should consider.

Finally, another CapEx upward revision is not off the table, considering the bottleneck that may be affecting the computing capacity required to serve their ads and to run the recommendation algorithms.

Overall, I expect the META share price to break all-time highs in the weeks after the Q2 earnings release.
